channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Mamdani Emerges as Kingmaker, Pushing His Slate to a Primary Sweep
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani shook the Democratic establishment by helping drive three progressive candidates to victory.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Fandos
item.media:credit: Todd Heisler/The New York Times
item.media:description: Brad Lander, center right, was one of the three congressional candidates backed by Mayor Zohran Mandani who won their primaries on Tuesday.

item.title: Insurgency Over Incumbency: 7 Takeaways From New York’s Primaries
item.description: All three congressional candidates who were backed by Mayor Zohran Mamdani won in Tuesday’s primary, bolstering his political movement.
item.dc:creator: Maya King, Sally Goldenberg and Emma Goldberg
item.media:credit: Bing Guan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani celebrated the victory of state Assemblywoman Claire Valdez, right, in a Democratic primary for a congressional seat.

item.title: Victories by Pro-Palestinian Democrats Show the Party’s Shift on Israel
item.description: Three Democrats who have been outspoken in their criticism of Israel won primaries in New York on Tuesday, signaling their party’s new skepticism of the country and its actions.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Lexi Parra/The New York Times
item.media:description: Darializa Avila Chevalier, who was endorsed by Mayor Zohran Mamdani of New York City, won an upset Democratic primary victory on Tuesday night over Representative Adriano Espaillat.

item.title: Schlossberg’s Defeat Dampens Dream of a Renewed Camelot
item.description: Jack Schlossberg fell well short of his bid to win a highly contested Democratic primary in Manhattan, his campaign hurt by staff defections and missed meetings.
item.dc:creator: Jesse McKinley and Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jack Schlossberg, with his father, Edwin Schlossberg, right, saw his early polling lead evaporate.

item.title: Deadly Heat Wave Grips Europe, and Congress Passes a Landmark Housing Bill
item.description: Plus, why some tech companies are telling workers to cut back on A.I.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Ian Stewart, Ségolène Le Stradic and Lisa Lerer
item.media:credit: Christophe Ena/Associated Press
item.media:description: Parisians bathe in the Canal Saint-Martin in Paris on Tuesday.

item.title: Housing Package Passed by Congress Has Wide Appeal, but It’s No Quick Fix
item.description: The first housing package in decades will take time to put into effect, testing the patience of families rankled by high prices.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, which cleared Congress on Tuesday, aims to increase new construction.

item.title: Iran’s Loyalists Promote a Wider Nationalism, Unveiled Women Included
item.description: Government supporters are showing off new ties with alleged former dissidents in a bid to show that they can withstand enemies at home as well as abroad.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon and Sanam Mahoozi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pro-government demonstration in Tehran last month where some of the women were partially veiled and some dressed more conservatively.

item.title: Trump Is Making Big Claims About the Iran Talks. Iran Keeps Contradicting Him.
item.description: President Trump appears to be describing his preferences as fully negotiated deals, in hopes of locking the Iranians in. The question is whether a succession of such disputes will sink the whole venture.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: In the negotiation between the United States and Iran, leaks are replaced by official, if fragmentary, announcements — usually from the American side.

item.title: Rubio Seeks to Reassure Persian Gulf Allies on Iran Deal
item.description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio was scheduled to meet with leaders in Abu Dhabi, Kuwait and Bahrain, which were targeted by Iranian attacks during the war.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/Reuters
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio greeting the Emirati ambassador to the U.S., Yousef al-Otaiba, in Abu Dhabi, U.A.E., on Tuesday.

item.title: Senate Votes to Direct End to Iran War, Rebuking Trump on War Powers
item.description: A few Republicans joined Democrats in favor of a measure instructing President Trump to halt military operations against Iran or seek congressional authorization.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Capitol seen on Tuesday. The vote came amid tensions over the war among the G.O.P., which faces a punishing political environment ahead of midterm elections.

item.title: Trump’s Pick for Top I.R.S. Lawyer Works at Firm That Represents Him
item.description: James R. Gadwood, the president’s nominee for chief counsel at the Internal Revenue Service, works at Miller & Chevalier, which has represented Mr. Trump in tax matters.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Internal Revenue Service building in Washington. James R. Gadwood is the second person President Trump has nominated to the agency’s top legal job in his second term.

item.title: Trump Says Vandals Sabotaged the Reflecting Pool. Internal Documents Raise Doubts.
item.description: The documents do not indicate that the peeling blue coating and algae blooms were caused intentionally.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and David A. Fahrenthold
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: The reflecting pool, seen from the Washington Monument, with workers attempting to contain the algae on Sunday.

item.title: Trump to Meet With G.O.P. Senators Amid New Divisions
item.description: The president has been unhappy with pushback from Republican senators and a resistance to abandoning the filibuster to pass new voting restrictions.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has been in a simmering feud with Senator John Thune, Republican of South Dakota and the majority leader, over a range of political and policy issues.

item.title: Alibaba Sues Pentagon Over China Military Label
item.description: The Chinese tech giant said it had no ties to China’s military and that the U.S. government had violated the law by making that claim.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Alibaba has become one of China’s leading artificial intelligence companies.

item.title: The Heat Wave in Europe Is Hitting Records. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: France had its hottest day ever, with forecasters in Britain predicting that the record for the highest temperature for this time of year was also likely to be broken.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar and Lynsey Chutel

item.title: Extreme Heat Across Europe Creates Divide Over Leaving Schools Open
item.description: Few schools in Britain or France have air-conditioning to keep children cool in soaring temperatures, leaving parents, teachers and officials divided about what to do.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:credit: Kevin Coombs/Reuters
item.media:description: Students standing near a fan at L’Ecole des Petits, a bilingual school in London on Wednesday.

item.title: Myanmar’s Civil War Has Become an Apocalypse
item.description: Overshadowed by conflicts in Ukraine, Gaza and Lebanon, the fighting in Myanmar’s isolated heartland has reached new depths.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Beech and Daniel Berehulak
item.category: Coups D'Etat and Attempted Coups D'Etat
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Daniel Berehulak/The New York Times
item.media:description: On a forgotten frontline, the rebels fight deep in the jungle.

item.title: What to Know About Myanmar’s Chaotic War
item.description: The Southeast Asian nation recently experienced its highest monthly civilian death toll since the war began in 2021.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Beech
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
Coups D'Etat and Attempted Coups D'Etat
item.media:credit: Daniel Berehulak/The New York Times
item.media:description: A rebel soldier at a frontline position in the Anyar region of Myanmar.

item.title: Welcome to the Luxury City Built by Taiwan’s A.I. Boom
item.description: Fortunes, luxury buildings and birthrates are rising in the city at the center of Taiwan’s chip supply chain.
item.dc:creator: Amy Chang Chien and Meaghan Tobin
item.media:credit: Lam Yik Fei for The New York Times
item.media:description: The shopping mall in Hsinchu, called Big City, ranks among Taiwan’s top retail centers.

item.title: A.I. Riches Fuel Economic Divide in Asia’s Chip Powerhouses
item.description: A.I. demand is driving stock market gains and booming exports in South Korea and Taiwan. But the rest of the economy is being left behind.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Catie Edmondson
item.media:credit: An Rong Xu for The New York Times

item.title: Asia Tech Shares Swing Wildly as A.I. Jitters Persist
item.description: The turbulence in Asia’s chip-dominated stock markets highlighted how heavily global equities have come to depend on enthusiasm for artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ahn Young-Joon/Associated Press

item.title: The Ukraine War Amputees Embracing Wake Boarding and Jujitsu
item.description: As the number of amputees in Ukraine soars, many are bonding by learning new sports, challenging both their bodies and their ideas of what they can do.
item.dc:creator: Siobhán O’Grady, Liubov Sholudko, Yurii Shyvala and Oksana Parafeniuk
item.media:credit: Oksana Parafeniuk for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ukrainian war veteran Volodymyr Kuzmenko tries wakeboarding for the first time in Kyiv, in May.

item.title: What’s Behind Broadway’s New Musical Drought?
item.description: Hollywood actors in starry plays, skittish investors and gate-keeping theater owners have all contributed to an unusually tough climate for song-and-dance shows.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.category: The Curious Case of Benjamin Button (Play)
Midnight in the Garden of Good and Evil (Play)
item.media:credit: Tim Lahan

item.title: Lions, Tigers and Bomb-Sniffing Dogs: Zoos Face Scores of Swatting Calls
item.description: About 40 zoos and aquariums across the United States have responded to bombing and shooting threats in recent months that turned out to be hoaxes.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: After the swatting episodes, Zoo Miami spent $1 million to update its security cameras and paid the Miami-Dade Sheriff’s Office for a security detail.

item.title: Thomas L. Friedman on the Clash at the Core of the Iran Deal
item.description: How the world views of Jared Kushner and the founder of the Islamic Republic of Iran can help explain the issue at the heart of the negotiations.
item.dc:creator: Thomas L. Friedman and Daniel J. Wakin
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times; source photo by Kevin Dietsch/Getty Images

item.title: Kevin Warsh Is Missing Alan Greenspan’s Point
item.description: Fed chair Kevin Warsh shouldn’t point to Alan Greenspan’s experience to bolster his argument that A.I. will allow the central bank to keep interest rates low.
item.dc:creator: David Wessel
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by The New York Times

item.title: Josh Johnson Muses on the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool
item.description: “Hey, kids, remember you wanted to go to Disneyland? Instead, we’re going to go see the world’s largest kombucha!” Johnson said of the pool renovation as a tourist stop.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Pregnant and Running for Re-election, a Democrat Faces U.S. Prosecution
item.description: Representative LaMonica McIver was charged with assault after an altercation with immigration agents that resulted in no injuries. She faces up to 17 years in prison.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Andres Kudacki for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative LaMonica McIver.
